claim.hypotheses: Individuals with lower executive function capacity (or lower Need for Cognition) will show a larger reward-induced reduction in task-switch costs than individuals with higher executive function capacity (or higher Need for Cognition).
claim.hypotheses_location: Page 1, right column, paragraph beginning “Considering these two separate lines of research… one possibility is that the mobilizing effects of reward incentives should be greater for lower-capacity individuals…”.
claim.statement: Lower executive function capacity and lower Need for Cognition predict larger increases in cognitive effort expenditure in response to monetary reward incentives, whereas individuals with greater executive function capacity or higher intrinsic motivation are less responsive to reward incentives.
claim.statement_location: Abstract, page 1, sentences beginning “In accordance with the predictions of a cost-benefit account of effort, we found that a low executive function capacity… predicted larger increase in cognitive effort expenditure in response to monetary reward incentives…”.

data.source: Laboratory experiment with computerised Stroop and task-switching paradigms plus questionnaires
data.access_details: Dataset available via Open Science Framework at http://osf.io/gqvsh
data.notes: Seven participants excluded for <80% accuracy and two for excessive missed deadlines; two more excluded from analyses involving NFC due to missing questionnaire data.

method.description: Participants first completed the Need for Cognition questionnaire and other scales, then a Stroop task to index executive function, followed by two counter-balanced blocks of a task-switching paradigm in which monetary reward for correct responses was High (5¢) or Low (1¢). Reaction times and accuracy were analysed with mixed-effects regressions to test whether reward effects on switch costs were moderated by executive function capacity and Need for Cognition.
steps: Administer questionnaires: Need for Cognition, BIS/BAS, Barratt Impulsiveness, GAD-7.
Run 1 practice block then 1 block of 120-trial computerized Stroop task (90 congruent, 30 incongruent).
Compute Stroop RT cost per participant (standardised incongruent − congruent RT).
Inform participants of performance-contingent bonuses and run two 140-trial task-switching blocks (High vs Low reward, order counter-balanced) with magnitude/parity tasks cued by card colour.
Insert filler task and 8-minute music break between blocks.
Exclude participants who fail accuracy/deadline criteria; final N = 45.
Log-transform correct-trial RTs; fit mixed-effects (lme4 in R) models: predictors = Trial type, Reward, Block, Stroop RT cost or NFC, and their interactions; logistic mixed models for accuracy.
Extract individual reward effects and interpret switch-cost modulation.
method.models: Linear mixed-effects regression for log RTs; logistic mixed-effects regression for accuracy
method.outcome_variable: Task-switch cost (difference in RT between switch and repeat trials); also response accuracy
method.independent_variables: Reward condition (High vs Low), Executive function capacity (Stroop RT cost), Need for Cognition score
method.control_variables: Trial type (switch vs repeat), Experimental block (practice/learning effects), Trial number

results.summary: Reward incentives reduced task-switch costs only for individuals with lower executive function capacity and for those low in Need for Cognition. A significant three-way interaction showed that the reward-induced switch-cost reduction grew as Stroop RT cost increased. Conversely, high-NFC participants showed no benefit (and slight cost) from higher reward, whereas low-NFC participants benefited substantially. No overall main effect of reward was detected at the group level, and accuracy patterns mirrored RT findings without significant interactions.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Three-way interaction (Trial type × Reward × Stroop RT cost) on log RT
Three-way interaction (Trial type × Reward × Need for Cognition) on log RT
Main effect of Reward on log RT

metadata.original_paper_title: Cognitive capacity limitations and Need for Cognition differentially predict reward-induced cognitive effort expenditure
